perturbation.original_text: XSPA shall operate and maintain the Stores with high standards of quality and service and shall at all times comply with any and all applicable laws in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: XSPA shall operate and maintain the Stores and strive to maintain quality and service.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the explicit requirement to comply with all applicable laws, creating a loophole. While the intention might still be to comply, the contract no longer mandates it, increasing the risk of non-compliance and potential legal challenges.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Business Law § 349 - Deceptive acts and practices unlawful
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the obligation to comply with all applicable laws, the modified text potentially opens XSPA up to claims of deceptive practices if they fail to comply with consumer protection laws, health and safety regulations, or other relevant statutes in operating the stores. The original text ensures compliance, but the modified version does not, which can be construed as misleading to Calm and potentially to consumers if issues arise.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with all applicable laws, whereas the changed text removes this explicit obligation, only requiring XSPA to 'strive to maintain quality and service'. This omission creates a potential contradiction with consumer protection laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 'Consumer Protection From Deceptive Acts and Practices,' indicating that businesses must adhere to certain consumer protection standards. By removing the explicit requirement to comply with all laws, XSPA opens itself to potential claims of deceptive practices if it fails to comply with these consumer protection laws. The original text provides an explicit safeguard. The omission of this safeguard creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The foregoing insurance policies shall name XSPA as the insured and Calm as additional insured (except for Workers' Compensation Insurance). If any such insurance is on a "claims made" basis, XSPA shall maintain coverage thereunder for a period of at least two (2) years following the termination of this Agreement. With respect to the foregoing, XSPA shall provide to Calm certificate(s) evidencing such insurance prior to or upon execution of this Agreement. The certificates shall provide that Calm will be given at least thirty (30) days prior written notice of cancellation or any material change in these policies.
perturbation.changed_text: The foregoing insurance policies shall name XSPA as the insured. Calm shall have no obligation to XSPA for the costs of insurance required, or for any other coverage that XSPA obtains, directly or indirectly for its own account.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for Calm to be named as an additional insured and eliminating the need for XSPA to provide certificates of insurance, eliminates a key consumer protection, as it limits Calm's ability to directly claim from XSPA's insurance for covered incidents, increasing legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Insurance Law § 3420 - Standard fire policy; contract; rights of mortgagee; prohibited provisions
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Insurance Law 3420 provides rights to injured parties to directly sue an insurer under certain circumstances. By eliminating Calm as an additional insured, this modification weakens their potential recourse in case of a covered incident, potentially contradicting the spirit, if not the exact letter, of the law which aims to protect parties from damages. Specifically, this section dictates the rights of injured parties to proceed directly against the insurer of the tortfeasor, and by removing the additional insured status for Calm, it creates a situation that might limit those rights depending on the specific claim.

2025-03-28 2025-05-16 Share Facebook Email SECTION 3420 Liability insurance; standard provisions; right of injured person Insurance (ISC) CHAPTER 28, ARTICLE 34 § 3420. Liability insurance; standard provisions; right of injured person. (a) No policy or contract insuring against liability for injury to person, except as provided in subsection (g) of this section, or against liability for injury to, or destruction of, property shall be issued or delivered in this state, unless it contains in substance the following provisions or provisions that are equally or more favorable to the insured and to judgment creditors so far as such provisions relate to judgment creditors: (1) A provision that the insolvency or bankruptcy of the person insured, or the insolvency of the insured's estate, shall not release the insurer from the payment of damages for injury sustained or loss occasioned during the life of and within the coverage of such policy or contract. (2) A provision that in case judgment against the insured or the insured's personal representative in an action brought to recover damages for injury sustained or loss or damage occasioned during the life of the policy or contract shall remain unsatisfied at the expiration of thirty days from the serving of notice of entry of judgment upon the attorney for the insured, or upon the insured, and upon the insurer, then an action may, except during a stay or limited stay of execution against the insured on such judgment, be maintained against the insurer under the terms of the policy or contract for the amount of such judgment not exceeding the amount of the applicable limit of coverage under such policy or contract. (3) A provision that notice given by or on behalf of the insured, or written notice by or on behalf of the injured person or any other claimant, to any licensed agent of the insurer in this state, with particulars sufficient to identify the insured, shall be deemed notice to the insurer. (4) A provision that failure to give any notice required to be given by such polic
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for Calm to be named as an additional insured on XSPA's insurance policies. This directly contradicts the intent of New York Insurance Law 3420, as supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which aims to protect injured parties by allowing them to directly sue an insurer under certain circumstances. By omitting Calm as an additional insured, the modification weakens Calm's potential recourse in case of a covered incident, thereby contradicting the law's objective of providing rights to injured parties.

perturbation.original_text: Calm shall prepare and maintain complete and accurate books and records covering all transactions relating to this Agreement. XSPA's representatives may, from time to time during regular business hours on reasonable advance notice, during the Term of this Agreement and for a period of six (6) months thereafter, inspect and audit such books and records and examine and copy all other documents and material in the possession or under the control of Calm with respect to the subject matter and the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Calm shall maintain books and records covering transactions relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes XSPA's right to inspect and audit Calm's books and records. This impairs XSPA's ability to verify the accuracy of payments and calculations, potentially leading to financial discrepancies that XSPA has no recourse to verify independently.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Commercial Code § 2-305 - Open Price Term
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly unrelated, the NY UCC 2-305 covers open price terms and requires good faith in setting prices. By removing XSPA's audit rights, the contract creates a situation where Calm could potentially manipulate records and breach the implied duty of good faith in determining the amount due to them under the agreement based on sales. XSPA has no means to independently verify sales amounts in order to determine if price and commission calculations were made in good faith. The audit provision is critical in ensuring compliance.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract previous SECTION 2-304 Price Payable in Money, Goods, Realty, or Otherwise up PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract next SECTION 2-306 Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-305 Open Price Term Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith. (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account.
perturbation.justification: The original contract included a provision allowing XSPA to inspect and audit Calm's books and records. The changed text removes this right, which creates a potential issue under NY UCC 2-305, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While snippet 1, referencing NY UCC 2-305, primarily discusses open price terms and good faith in setting prices, the Law Explanation correctly argues that removing audit rights impairs XSPA's ability to verify if price and commission calculations were made in good faith. The Omission Legal Contradiction definition states that a contradiction exists when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant. While not directly a 'consumer' protection, the audit provision serves as a control to ensure compliance with good faith pricing, a component of the law. This creates a legally vulnerable situation where Calm could manipulate records without independent verification by XSPA.
